evidence: ui/src/lib/stores/websocket.js defines a very large command facade (dozens of exported functions like `toggleWifi`, `bootShip`, `restoreBackup`, `installGallseg`) that mostly forward to `sendTypeCommand`/`sendActionCommand`/`sendUrbitCommand` with minimal added behavior.

The same module hides two different transport contracts behind one `sendCommand` call path: URBIT mode calls `sendPoke(payload)`, non-URBIT mode wraps payload with generated `id`/optional `token` and sends over WebSocket.

Bundled routing layer adds many low-leverage proxy modules (`goseg/web/_app/immutable/chunks/1.0d44cf41.js`, `10.35bd59a0.js`, `11.9463674b.js`, `12.0b2f13c7.js`, `3.1bf9a0f6.js`) that only re-export entries/components while `entry/app.e0ffcb44.js` orchestrates a deep import graph.

goseg/web/_app/immutable/chunks/clipboard.750479b4.js embeds clipboard.js v2.0.11 and still executes `document.execCommand(...)` and `document.queryCommandSupported(...)`, which are legacy clipboard paths.
goseg/web/_app/immutable/chunks/clipboard.750479b4.js has no `navigator.clipboard` path, so copy/cut behavior is tied to older browser command APIs rather than a modern-first migration with fallback.

goseg/web/_app/immutable/chunks/sigil-js.esm.0c91ed14.js embeds `react.production.min.js` with `React v17.0.2`/`f.version="17.0.2"`, indicating a retained legacy framework runtime inside the current frontend bundle.

ui/src/lib/stores/websocket.js exposes many exported command functions (e.g., login, setupStarTram, setPackSchedule, submitReport) that are synchronous wrappers but all route through async sendCommand, so callers cannot distinguish fire-and-forget from awaitable/failable operations.

ui/src/lib/stores/data.js hard-codes URBIT_MODE and DEV_PANEL writable defaults, while ui/vite.config.js defines GS_URBIT_MODE and GS_DEV_PANEL environment values, creating conflicting configuration surfaces.

ui/src/lib/stores/websocket.js has public API fan-out through sendTypeCommand/sendActionCommand/sendSystemCommand/sendSetupCommand/sendStartramCommand/sendUrbitCommand/sendDevCommand but no explicit contract on success/failure semantics for any exported command.

ui/src/lib/stores/websocket.js safeParseSocketMessage catches JSON.parse errors and returns null, silently dropping inbound messages after logging; this differs from outbound paths that allow throw/reject behavior.
ui/src/lib/stores/websocket.js sendCommand calls SESSION.send without readyState/SESSION existence checks or try/catch, so transport failures can surface as uncaught runtime exceptions.
ui/src/lib/stores/websocket.js reconnect loops via setTimeout(()=>connect(url),1000) and SESSION.onerror only logs to console; failures are not normalized into a shared error channel/store.

goseg/web/_app/immutable/entry/app.e0ffcb44.js uses a global handleError hook that only console.error logs, which reinforces log-only error handling instead of consistent user-facing or structured propagation.

findings.summary: Single websocket store acts as a low-leverage pass-through API hub across unrelated domains.
evidence: Most exported functions in `websocket.js` are one-line wrappers to generic send helpers, but are grouped for auth/setup/system/startram/urbit/support/logs/penpai/dev in one module.
`(home)-page.svelte.3cdaa764.js` and `_layout.svelte.8a290471.js` consume this central command surface (via bundled websocket imports), reinforcing high coupling to a single command hub.
The abstraction adds naming convenience but little policy/validation at boundaries, so module size and coupling grow without proportional behavior encapsulation.
findings.suggestion: Split command creation by domain (e.g., `commands/system`, `commands/urbit`, `commands/startram`) with a shared typed transport adapter; keep route imports domain-scoped so each page depends on only its command set.

findings.summary: One send abstraction hides incompatible URBIT and websocket semantics behind identical call sites.
evidence: `sendCommand` branches on `URBIT_MODE`: one path calls `sendPoke(payload)`, the other path mutates request shape with generated `id`/`token` and sends raw WebSocket JSON.

Ack/error handling in `handleActivity` depends on websocket `id`/`response` semantics, while `urbit.f551ce98.js` implements separate poke/subscribe event handling and payload translation (`json:{action: JSON.stringify(...)}`), indicating distinct protocol behavior.
Callers use the same high-level command wrappers without explicit transport capability/guarantees, so abstraction hides meaningful contract differences.
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit transport adapters with a shared normalized result contract (success, nack, parse_error, auth_error) and make command layer consume that contract instead of branching inside `sendCommand`.

findings.summary: Generated route/component proxy chunks add indirection depth without local behavioral value.
evidence: Several assigned chunk files are one-line re-export shims (`import default ...; export {t as component};`) and do not add policy, translation, or isolation.
`entry/app.e0ffcb44.js` then assembles these proxies into a large route dictionary/import graph, increasing abstraction depth for readers debugging runtime behavior from committed artifacts.
findings.suggestion: Do not treat immutable build artifacts as primary abstraction boundaries; keep source modules as the maintained abstraction layer and exclude/reduce committed proxy artifacts where possible (or isolate them to a generated-only delivery path).

findings.summary: Clipboard handling remains on deprecated command APIs without a modern Clipboard API migration path.
evidence: clipboard chunk calls `document.execCommand("copy")` / `document.execCommand("cut")` in copy/cut flows.
clipboard chunk support check relies on `document.queryCommandSupported(...)`.
no `navigator.clipboard` usage appears in the clipboard chunk, so migration to async clipboard APIs is incomplete.
findings.suggestion: Upgrade or replace the vendored clipboard dependency with a modern implementation that uses `navigator.clipboard` as primary path and keeps legacy fallback only where required by an explicit browser support policy.

findings.summary: A legacy React 17 runtime is still bundled, suggesting migration residue and dual-stack maintenance debt.
evidence: sigil chunk contains `/** @license React v17.0.2` and `react.production.min.js` text.
sigil chunk includes `f.version="17.0.2"`, confirming pinned legacy runtime code in active bundle output.
same batch also shows other legacy third-party residue (clipboard.js v2.0.11), indicating a broader incomplete dependency/runtime modernization pattern.
findings.suggestion: Audit the dependency introducing `sigil-js` runtime code, then migrate to a framework-agnostic or current-version build artifact (or isolate it behind a separately loaded compatibility boundary) so core frontend bundles do not carry old runtime stacks.

findings.summary: Outbound websocket commands can fail with uncaught exceptions because send paths do not enforce connection state or normalize failures.
evidence: ui/src/lib/stores/websocket.js: sendCommand executes SESSION.send(JSON.stringify(data)) without checking SESSION existence or SESSION.readyState.
ui/src/lib/stores/websocket.js: SESSION.onerror only does console.log(error), and reconnect schedules another connect without structured failure handling.
goseg/web/_app/immutable/entry/app.e0ffcb44.js: top-level handleError hook only logs with console.error, so runtime faults are not converted into consistent application error state.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single transport error contract: gate send operations on OPEN readyState, wrap send failures into a typed error/result channel, and update shared stores so callers/UI can handle retry/auth/session faults consistently instead of relying on console logs.

findings.summary: Inbound parse failures are swallowed by returning null, causing silent message loss and inconsistent failure behavior versus other paths.
evidence: ui/src/lib/stores/websocket.js: safeParseSocketMessage catches JSON.parse error, logs at most once per 5s, and returns null.
ui/src/lib/stores/websocket.js: connect onmessage only calls handleMessage when parsed is truthy; malformed frames are dropped without stateful error signaling.
goseg/web/_app/immutable/entry/app.e0ffcb44.js: global error hook is log-only, so malformed inbound payload conditions are not surfaced through a consistent user-observable channel.
findings.suggestion: Replace null-swallow behavior with explicit parse-failure events (e.g., error store or typed message result), include payload metadata, and route through the same error handling surface used for transport and command failures.

findings.summary: Runtime feature/config API is split between injected env defines and hard-coded writable defaults, making behavior source-of-truth unclear.
evidence: ui/vite.config.js defines process.env.GS_URBIT_MODE and process.env.GS_DEV_PANEL values from environment.
ui/src/lib/stores/data.js sets URBIT_MODE = writable(true) and DEV_PANEL = writable(false) with comments referencing config, but does not read the injected values.
This creates two competing API surfaces for the same settings (build-time env define vs runtime hard-coded store defaults).
findings.suggestion: Establish one configuration boundary: initialize stores from a single config module that reads injected env values once (with validation/defaults), and remove hard-coded divergent defaults from feature stores.

findings.summary: Public command API shape hides async/failure semantics by exposing mostly synchronous wrappers over async transport.
evidence: ui/src/lib/stores/websocket.js: exported commands (e.g., login, bootShip, submitReport, setPackSchedule) return nothing and delegate to async sendCommand.
ui/src/lib/stores/websocket.js: sendCommand itself awaits async token/id generation and transport send, but the wrapper API does not return a Promise/result to callers.

goseg/web/_app/immutable/entry/boot-new-page.svelte.3dc93e94.js is one of multiple generated route entries consuming websocket command exports, so ambiguous command contracts propagate broadly across UI routes.
findings.suggestion: Define a consistent command interface (e.g., Promise<Result> or explicit fire-and-forget naming split) and apply it across all exported websocket actions so callers can reliably reason about completion, ack, and failure handling.
